summary: DHH outlines five core values driving 37signals' decision to leave cloud infrastructure, going beyond the cost argument he's previously made. The values center on independence from hyperscalers, serving the open internet, fiscal responsibility, industry leadership, and maintaining entrepreneurial adventure. He frames the cloud exit as both a practical business decision and an ideological stance against the consolidation of internet infrastructure. The post positions 37signals as pioneers showing other SaaS companies that owning hardware is superior to renting it.
key_points: Cloud dependency violates 37signals' core value of independence, making them subject to Amazon's pricing decisions
Consolidating servers under a few hyperscalers erodes the open, decentralized nature of the internet
For established SaaS businesses, renting cloud infrastructure is fiscally irresponsible when you're paying purchase price every few months in rent
The cloud-as-default narrative for SaaS companies is wrong — most businesses of sufficient size should own their infrastructure
Long-running companies need ambitious projects to maintain energy and avoid stagnation
money_quotes: Being trapped in Amazon's cloud, and having to suffer the indignities of outrageous pricing for new experiments, has become an intolerable violation of this core value.
We will not contribute to the erosion of those ideals by voting with our dollars for further consolidation of the servers that run this beautiful free haven into the hands of a few hyperscalers.
We're paying as much to rent the donkey every few months as it would cost to buy it. When that's the case, you should own the donkey!
Cloud has been sold as The Answer to SaaS companies like ours for a decade or more. I bought the narrative. We bought the narrative. The narrative is not true.
Most SaaS business of our size and above should be owning their infrastructure rather than renting it.
To keep the fire alive, we should continue to set our standards high, keep our values honest, and press forward into new territories on all fronts. Otherwise we will shrivel.
key_insight: Leaving the cloud is not just a cost optimization but a values-driven decision about independence, internet stewardship, and refusing to fund the consolidation of infrastructure under a few dominant players.
